question: Will Rumen Radev be the next prime minister of Bulgaria after the 2026 parliamentary election?

fragility_points: Candidate-specific current-cycle evidence was weaker and less independently confirmed than ideal.
If Radev's coalition vehicle is stronger than this run could verify, the estimate is too low.
key_assumptions: Radev does not currently have clearly verified coalition-dominant parliamentary backing.
The next counted prime minister will emerge through ordinary post-election coalition bargaining.
Caretaker prime ministers remain excluded under the contract as written.
main_logical_chain: Start from the outside view: fragmented parliamentary systems rarely justify near-certainty on one named post-election PM without hard coalition evidence.
Check the contract: only the next officially sworn-in PM counts, and caretaker PMs do not count.
Check structural mechanics: Bulgarian PM selection runs through parliamentary coalition support, not presidential prominence alone.
Check current context: repeated instability keeps alternative outcomes and delay risk live.
Net result: Radev can be a contender, but 90.35% is too high on the evidence reviewed.
payload.main_thesis: The market is materially overconfident on Rumen Radev; Bulgaria's fragmented parliamentary structure and caretaker exclusion make a 90%+ estimate too high without stronger direct coalition evidence.
quote_anchors: caretaker prime ministers do not count
officially sworn in as Prime Minister of Bulgaria
My current estimate is 35% for Radev
payload.source_quality_view: Primary source-of-truth logic is relatively clear via the contract and official government surface, but candidate-specific evidence independence was only medium-low.
strongest_disconfirmers: Current public summaries suggest Radev may lead or be associated with a real political vehicle in the 2026 cycle, which could make his path more real than a pure ex-president narrative.
strongest_supports: Bulgaria is a parliamentary republic where prime ministers are produced by parliamentary majority or coalition bargaining.
Repeated elections and coalition failures since 2021 reduce confidence in any single named outcome.
The contract excludes caretaker prime ministers, removing an easy confusion path.
payload.timing_relevance: Election date was verified as 2026-04-19 in contextual sources, and the contract deadline runs to 2027-03-31 11:59 PM ET, leaving meaningful time for post-election bargaining or deadlock.
unresolved_ambiguities: Whether Radev actually leads a coalition-capable electoral vehicle in 2026.
Whether deadlock could push resolution to Other by the contract deadline.
payload.what_would_change_view: Independent confirmation that Radev leads a coalition-capable bloc or is the focal post-election compromise candidate would move the estimate up materially; evidence of prolonged deadlock would move it down and raise Other.

fragility_points: Accessible polling / seat-projection evidence was limited.
Media context on Radev may share overlapping underlying sourcing.
A sudden coalition agreement could move the fair value sharply upward.
key_assumptions: Radev remains the strongest single contender into and just after election day.
Coalition formation and investiture risk in Bulgaria remain nontrivial even if Radev finishes first.
No pre-election coalition lock already makes Radev overwhelmingly certain to be sworn in.
main_logical_chain: Radev appears to be the leading named contender and likely focal winner of market attention.
But the contract excludes caretaker PMs and requires formal swearing-in after the election.
That makes coalition bargaining and nomination path the decisive catalyst, not election day headlines alone.
So Radev should be favored, but not priced like a near lock.
payload.main_thesis: Rumen Radev is the leading contender, but formal post-election coalition formation and swearing-in risk make him materially less than a 90%+ certainty to be the next sworn-in Bulgarian prime minister.
quote_anchors: The market resolves on the next individual who is officially sworn in as Prime Minister of Bulgaria following the next parliamentary election.
Any interim or caretaker Prime Minister will not count toward the resolution of this market.
Prime Minister Andrey Gurov: Elections are an investment in trust between citizens and institutions
payload.source_quality_view: High for contract wording and official caretaker-PM status, medium for Radev electoral-strength context, with medium overall source independence and medium source-of-truth ambiguity before formal swearing-in.
strongest_disconfirmers: The contract resolves only on the next person formally sworn in as regular PM, not on poll leadership or plurality.
Bulgaria's repeated snap-election fragmentation creates material risk that Radev does not convert electoral strength into investiture.
If no qualifying PM is appointed by 2027-03-31 ET, the market resolves Other.
strongest_supports: Recent reporting shows Radev resigned, entered the race through Progressive Bulgaria, and is treated as a major contender.
Official Bulgarian government releases confirm the country is still under a caretaker PM, so the resolving PM has yet to emerge from the post-election process.
Radev is the most plausible focal person for the coalition-formation sequence after the election.
payload.timing_relevance: The key repricing window is immediately after the 19 Apr 2026 election, when seat math and coalition bargaining determine whether Radev can actually be sworn in.
unresolved_ambiguities: Exact seat distribution after the election.
Which parties would back or tolerate a Radev-led cabinet.
How quickly a regular post-election government can be sworn in.
payload.what_would_change_view: Multiple independent reports or official signals that Radev has secured a governing majority and is on a clear path to quick investiture would push the estimate higher; evidence of coalition refusal or renewed deadlock would push it lower.

fragility_points: Heavy dependence on contextual rather than authoritative primary evidence for Radev's path.
Outcome turns on post-election coalition sequencing, not just candidacy or popularity.
key_assumptions: Public reporting that Radev resigned and entered the 2026 race is broadly correct.
Radev remains the central post-election coalition focal point.
No rival coalition figure is sworn in first as non-caretaker PM.
main_logical_chain: Start from the 90.35% market price as an information-rich prior.
Verify contract mechanics: only the next formally sworn non-caretaker PM counts.
Confirm that public reporting makes Radev a plausible contender rather than a category error.
Discount the price because publicly auditable evidence is not strong enough for near-certainty in a coalition-dependent system.
Land at 65%: favorite, but materially below market.
payload.main_thesis: Radev is a plausible favorite, but the market's 90.35% price looks too high relative to publicly auditable evidence for a still-contingent coalition and swearing-in process.
quote_anchors: Primary: official information from the Government of Bulgaria.
payload.source_quality_view: Official contract and government context are useful, but the core pro-Radev thesis still relied materially on lower-independence public contextual sources; source-of-truth ambiguity is medium.
strongest_disconfirmers: No authoritative Bulgarian source reviewed here directly establishes Radev as the dominant path to being next sworn PM.
Parliamentary coalition bargaining and formal swearing-in remain meaningful contingencies.
strongest_supports: Contract excludes caretaker prime ministers, making the current caretaker officeholder less relevant.
Public election reporting says Radev resigned and formed Progressive Bulgaria to contest the election.
Institutional logic supports markets focusing on coalition focal points before swearing-in.
payload.timing_relevance: High: the election is scheduled for 19 April 2026, while the contract is explicitly about the next post-election sworn non-caretaker PM and has a long-stop to March 31, 2027.
unresolved_ambiguities: Authoritative confirmation of Radev's exact candidacy / coalition status.
Which parties would back or block a Radev-led government.
How quickly an official swearing-in would occur after the election.
payload.what_would_change_view: I would move up on authoritative Bulgarian evidence or strong independent reporting showing Radev has a dominant coalition path; I would move down on evidence another coalition leader is more likely to be sworn first or that deadlock makes Other more live.

fragility_points: No reviewed source showed a secured coalition agreement for a Radev-led cabinet.
A rival coalition could form around another figure.
Extended deadlock could push the market toward another person or 'Other'.
key_assumptions: Progressive Bulgaria remains near the top of the field on election day.
At least one viable coalition path to a Radev-led government exists.
Deadlock or an exclusion coalition does not block formal swearing-in.
main_logical_chain: Radev is eligible and actively running after resigning the presidency.
He appears to be the leading named contender entering the election.
But the market contract requires a formally sworn-in PM and excludes caretaker outcomes.
In a fragmented parliamentary system, coalition arithmetic can block a favorite from being sworn in.
Therefore Radev remains the likeliest individual outcome, but well below the market's 90.35% confidence.
payload.main_thesis: Rumen Radev is a plausible leading contender to become Bulgaria's next prime minister, but the market overstates certainty because coalition formation and formal swearing-in risk remain material.
quote_anchors: much depends on the arithmetic in the National Assembly
Any interim or caretaker Prime Minister will not count toward the resolution
payload.source_quality_view: Adequate high-difficulty coverage: market contract plus an official Bulgarian institutional source, one current newswire source, and independent contextual sources; main weakness is that coalition viability remains inferred rather than directly confirmed.
strongest_disconfirmers: The contract resolves on formal swearing-in, not electoral leadership.
Bulgaria's repeated coalition failures raise deadlock risk.
Contextual reporting suggests Radev's bloc may lead with only around one-fifth of the vote, far from a stand-alone majority.
strongest_supports: Radev resigned the presidency and is actively leading Progressive Bulgaria into the election.
Multiple sources describe Progressive Bulgaria as a leading electoral vehicle.
Radev is publicly positioning himself to form a government, not just contest the election.
payload.timing_relevance: Election is April 19, 2026; official reporting and assembly-convening timelines create a meaningful gap between election day and any qualifying swearing-in, while the market falls back to Other if no qualifying PM is appointed by March 31, 2027 ET.
unresolved_ambiguities: Actual seat distribution after threshold effects.
Which parties would back or veto Radev as PM.
How quickly a non-caretaker government can be formed after the election.
payload.what_would_change_view: Credible evidence of a workable coalition path or partner commitments would move the estimate up; coalition vetoes, poll erosion, or signs that another figure has the cleaner investiture path would move it down.

fragility_points: Limited direct independent reporting access in this run.
Local-language or trader information could justify a higher estimate.
If Radev is already the de facto coalition compromise, this view is too low.
key_assumptions: Radev's political salience is being overweighted relative to formal investiture mechanics.
Coalition-formation risk remains meaningful after the April 19, 2026 election.
No currently accessible authoritative source already locks in Radev as sworn-in PM.
main_logical_chain: Start from the 90.35% market-implied baseline.
Audit the contract and note that only a formally sworn-in post-election PM counts.
In a parliamentary system, coalition and investiture mechanics create more failure paths than a simple name-recognition market may imply.
Therefore Radev remains favored but not at near-certainty; estimate 72%.
payload.main_thesis: The market is too confident on Rumen Radev because the contract resolves on formal post-election swearing-in, excludes caretaker outcomes, and leaves meaningful coalition/deadlock risk.
quote_anchors: officially sworn in as Prime Minister
Any interim or caretaker Prime Minister will not count
If no such Prime Minister is appointed by March 31, 2027 ... resolve to Other
payload.source_quality_view: Good for contract interpretation and institutional mechanism, weaker for independent event-level confirmation; enough for a rule-sensitive variant view but not for high-confidence contrarianism.
strongest_disconfirmers: The market itself implies 90.35%, suggesting strong consensus or local information in Radev's favor.
Radev is an obvious focal political figure and may indeed be the dominant realistic candidate.
strongest_supports: Contract requires the first person officially sworn in as PM after the election.
Caretaker or interim PMs explicitly do not count.
The contract allows an 'Other' resolution if no qualifying PM is appointed by March 31, 2027.
payload.timing_relevance: The election is scheduled for April 19, 2026, while the contract resolves on the next qualifying sworn-in PM and falls back to Other on March 31, 2027 11:59 PM ET.
unresolved_ambiguities: Whether independent Bulgarian reporting already converges on Radev as the coalition PM choice.
How much the market price reflects inaccessible high-quality local information rather than narrative anchoring.
payload.what_would_change_view: Official government confirmation of Radev's install path or multiple independent reports of a settled coalition around him would move the estimate materially upward.
